items.q: When does an electron behave as a wave?
items.correct: In experiments that probe wave properties (e.g. diffraction through a crystal); in particle-like experiments it behaves as a particle.
items.misconception: Electrons are always waves and only look like particles at low energy.
items.random_wrong: Electrons are sometimes waves and sometimes particles depending on the temperature of the apparatus.
items.why: Students seek a single 'true' description; the modern view is that the experimental context selects which behaviour is observed.

items.q: When you rub a balloon and stick it to the wall, what has been transferred?
items.correct: Electrons have been transferred between the balloon and the cloth (not the wall).
items.misconception: Protons jumped from the balloon to the wall, causing static cling.
items.random_wrong: Charge was created by friction and pulled the balloon to the wall.
items.why: Students don't know that nuclei (and protons) are essentially immobile in everyday situations — only electrons move in static electricity.

items.q: Does Hooke's law apply to a spring for any amount of stretching?
items.correct: No — it only holds up to the limit of proportionality; beyond that the relationship between force and extension is no longer linear.
items.misconception: Yes — Hooke's law applies to any spring at any extension.
items.random_wrong: Only when the force is small enough to be ignored.
items.why: Students apply F = kx universally without noting the limit of proportionality, leading to over-prediction at large extensions.

items.q: Two coherent waves in phase undergo:
items.why: Correct answer: Constructive interference. “Destructive interference” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Young's slits: λ = 600 nm, a = 0.2 mm, D = 2 m. Fringe spacing in mm:
items.why: The real figure is 6. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
